metadata.title: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Classical waves predict a scattered X-ray keeps its wavelength, but Compton measured a real shift from 0.071 to 0.073 nanometers. Treating the photon as a particle carrying momentum p equals h over lambda turns the event into a billiard-ball collision. The photon gives energy to the recoiling electron, so it leaves with less energy and therefore a longer wavelength. The shift depends only on the scattering angle: zero straight through, one Compton wavelength at 90 degrees, and double at 180.

artifactLines: Classical waves predict a scattered X-ray keeps its wavelength, but Compton measured a real shift from 0.071 to 0.073 nanometers.
Treating the photon as a particle carrying momentum p equals h over lambda turns the event into a billiard-ball collision.
The photon gives energy to the recoiling electron, so it leaves with less energy and therefore a longer wavelength.
The shift depends only on the scattering angle: zero straight through, one Compton wavelength at 90 degrees, and double at 180.

beats.narration_text: The jump from 0.071 to 0.073 nanometers at 90 degrees, matching the 2.426 picometer Compton wavelength, made photon momentum feel real to me. I want to run the collision math. Help me: (1) use delta-lambda equals h over m-e-c times one minus cosine theta to get the shift at 0, 90, and 180 degrees; (2) confirm the 4.85 picometer doubling at back-scatter; (3) compute the kinetic energy handed to the recoiling electron at 90 degrees.

props.segment: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons
props.command: The jump from 0.071 to 0.073 nanometers at 90 degrees, matching the 2.426 picometer Compton wavelength, made photon momentum feel real to me. I want to run the collision math. Help me: (1) use delta-lambda equals h over m-e-c times one minus cosine theta to get the shift at 0, 90, and 180 degrees; (2) confirm the 4.85 picometer doubling at back-scatter; (3) compute the kinetic energy handed to the recoiling electron at 90 degrees.

props.segment: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: classical wave theory prediction for X-ray scattering — incident wave shakes electron, re-emits at same frequency, no wavelength shift"

beats.narration_text: Classical wave theory says this is impossible. A wave hitting an electron should shake the electron and re-emit radiation at the same frequency. The wavelength should not change. That prediction is clear and it is wrong.

props.headline: Classical wave theory says this is impossible. A wave hitting an…

beats.narration_text: Compton's 1923 measurement: X-rays at 0.071 nanometers scattered from graphite at 90 degrees come out at 0.073 nanometers. Two point four picometers longer. Repeatable. Classical physics gives zero shift.

props.headline: Compton's 1923 measurement: X-rays at 0.071 nanometers scattered from graphite at…

card.copy:
Incoming: 0.071 nm
Scattered at 90°: 0.073 nm
Classical prediction: no shift. Why the shift?

props.segment: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: photon as billiard ball — momentum p = h/lambda, photon collides with electron and transfers energy and momentum like a particle"

beats.narration_text: The answer: treat the photon as a particle carrying momentum p equals h over lambda. Now scatter it off the electron like a billiard-ball collision. The photon gives some of its energy to the recoiling electron — and less energy means longer wavelength.

props.headline: The answer: treat the photon as a particle carrying momentum p…

props.segment: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: Compton formula delta-lambda = (h/mec)(1 - cos theta) — show Compton wavelength 2.426 pm, plot delta-lambda vs scattering angle from 0 to 180 degrees"

beats.narration_text: The shift depends only on the scattering angle. At 90 degrees, delta-lambda equals the Compton wavelength of the electron: 2.426 picometers. That constant is h over m-sub-e times c. It is a fundamental length scale set by the electron's mass.

props.headline: The shift depends only on the scattering angle. At 90 degrees,…

props.segment: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: Compton scattering at 0, 90, 180 degrees — show photon and recoil arrows, label delta-lambda = 0, 2.426 pm, 4.852 pm for each case"

beats.narration_text: At 0 degrees — straight through — the shift is zero: the photon barely grazes the electron. At 180 degrees — bouncing straight back — the shift is double the Compton wavelength: 4.85 picometers. The formula captures every angle.

props.headline: At 0 degrees — straight through — the shift is zero:…

props.segment: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: electron recoil at 90 degree Compton scatter — incoming X-ray 0.071 nm, scattered 0.073 nm, 0.24 keV transferred to recoiling electron"

beats.narration_text: The energy transferred to the electron at 90 degrees is 0.24 kiloelectronvolts. That recoiling electron carries kinetic energy. This is not a thermal effect or an instrumental artifact — it is a direct measurement of light acting like a particle.

props.headline: The energy transferred to the electron at 90 degrees is 0.24…

props.segment: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: Compton 1923 data — X-rays scattered from graphite at multiple angles, measured wavelength shift matching the formula, classical prediction at zero shift"

beats.narration_text: Compton published in 1923. Classical physics had no answer. Wave theory could not produce a wavelength shift. The only model that matched was photon-as-particle with momentum. Compton received the Nobel Prize in 1927.

props.headline: Compton published in 1923. Classical physics had no answer. Wave theory…

props.segment: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: photoelectric and Compton effects as dual proof of photon momentum — both require p = h/lambda, show the two experiments side by side"

beats.narration_text: The Compton effect sits alongside the photoelectric effect as proof that light carries discrete momentum. Together they make the same point: energy and momentum in a light beam come in packets. They are not continuous. The wave picture captures the distribution; the particle picture captures the collision.

props.headline: The Compton effect sits alongside the photoelectric effect as proof that…

beats.narration_text: X-rays change wavelength because photons carry momentum. The shift is Δλ = (h/mₑc)(1−cosθ). At 90° the shift is 2.4 pm. Classical waves cannot do this. Compton 1923.

props.headline: X-rays change wavelength because photons carry momentum. The shift is Δλ…

card.copy:
Photon momentum: p = h/λ.
Shift Δλ = (h/mₑc)(1−cosθ).
At 90°: Δλ = 2.4 pm (Compton 1923).

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Five angles. Ask Claude to calculate the shift for each and plot the curve. Every point confirms the same billiard-ball momentum.

props.segment: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons
props.command: calculate the Compton wavelength shift for X-rays at 0.071 nm scattered at 30, 60, 90, 120, and 150 degrees — then plot scattered wavelength vs scattering angle
